sections.title: React.memo and Object Props
sections.description: React.memo does a shallow comparison of props to decide whether to re-render a component. This works fine for primitive values — strings, numbers, booleans. But it breaks down silently when you pass an object or a function as a prop.
paragraphs: Every time a component renders, any object literal written inline is a new reference in memory. Shallow comparison checks references, not deep equality — so React.memo sees a "changed" prop and re-renders anyway, defeating its purpose entirely.

subsections.heading: The problem
Fix 1 — stabilize with useMemo
subsections.content: Wrap the object in useMemo so its reference only changes when its dependencies change.

subsections.heading: Fix 2 — define the value outside the component
subsections.content: If the value is static and never changes, move it outside the component entirely. No hook needed.

sections.title: React.memo and Function Props
sections.description: The same issue applies to functions. An arrow function written inline creates a new reference on every render. Passing it as a prop to a memoized child means that child will always re-render.

subsections.heading: The problem
Fix — stabilize with useCallback
subsections.content: useCallback memoizes the function itself, returning the same reference between renders as long as its dependencies haven't changed.

sections.title: Custom Comparators in React.memo
sections.description: React.memo accepts a second argument: a comparison function. It receives the previous and next props, and lets you decide whether the component should re-render. Return true to skip re-render (props are equal), false to allow it.
paragraphs: This is useful when you want to do a partial or semantic comparison — for example, comparing only the id of a user object rather than the whole object.

sections.title: The mental model
sections.description: React.memo is only as good as the stability of the props you pass. Before reaching for memoization, ask: are my props stable references?
paragraphs: Primitive values (string, number, boolean) — stable by value. React.memo works as expected.
Objects passed inline — new reference every render. Use useMemo or lift them outside.
Functions passed inline — new reference every render. Use useCallback.

sections.title: React Strict Mode
sections.description: React.StrictMode is a development-only tool. It doesn't render any visible UI, but it activates extra checks and warnings for its descendants.
paragraphs: One behavior that catches people off guard: in Strict Mode, React intentionally mounts components twice in development. This means your useEffect will run, its cleanup will run, then it will run again — all on the initial mount.

This is intentional. React is verifying that your effects are resilient and that your cleanup logic correctly undoes what the effect sets up. If your app breaks with Strict Mode's double-invocation, it means your cleanup is incomplete — not that Strict Mode is wrong.

subsections.code:
import { StrictMode } from 'react';

<StrictMode>
  <App />
</StrictMode>

sections.title: useEffect vs useLayoutEffect
sections.description: Both hooks run after render, but at different times. The difference is subtle but matters for visual consistency.

subsections.heading: useEffect — after paint
subsections.content: Runs asynchronously after the browser has painted the screen. The user sees the updated UI before the effect fires. Ideal for data fetching, subscriptions, logging — anything that doesn't need to block painting.

subsections.heading: useLayoutEffect — before paint
subsections.content: Runs synchronously after React has updated the DOM, but before the browser paints. Useful when you need to measure the DOM or make adjustments that would cause a visible flicker if done after paint. Prefer useEffect by default. Reach for useLayoutEffect only when you observe a flash or need DOM measurements before the paint.

sections.title: await and State Update Batching
sections.description: React batches multiple state updates into a single re-render when they happen in the same synchronous event handler. React 18 extended this to async contexts too — but there's a catch: await can break batching.
paragraphs: Any state updates that occur after an await expression are no longer in the same microtask and may not be batched together, causing separate re-renders.

subsections.heading: The problem
Fix — restructure or use flushSync

sections.title: Derived State from Props
sections.description: A common anti-pattern is copying a prop into state and then syncing it with a useEffect. This creates an unnecessary extra render cycle and can lead to subtle bugs where state lags behind the prop.

subsections.heading: The anti-pattern
The best solution
subsections.content: If the value is derived purely from props, compute it during render. Only reach for state if the value genuinely diverges from the prop over time due to user interaction.

sections.title: Abort Controllers and Race Conditions
sections.description: When you fetch data inside a useEffect, the fetch continues even if the component unmounts before it completes. Calling setState on an unmounted component produces a warning: "Can't perform a React state update on an unmounted component."
paragraphs: There's also a race condition: if the effect re-runs (e.g. when id changes), a slow earlier fetch could resolve after a faster later one, overwriting your state with stale data.

subsections.heading: The problem
Fix — use an AbortController
subsections.content: Pass the controller's signal to fetch and abort it in the cleanup function. This cancels the in-flight request and prevents stale state updates.

sections.title: Controlled Components
sections.description: A controlled component is one where React owns the form element's value. The input's value is driven by state, and every keystroke updates that state via an onChange handler. This gives you a single source of truth.

subsections.heading: Uncontrolled vs controlled
Common pitfall
subsections.content: Initializing state with undefined makes the input uncontrolled. When state later becomes a string, React warns about switching from uncontrolled to controlled. Always initialize with an empty string.

sections.title: Lesser-known facts about useReducer
sections.description: Most people know useReducer as a more structured alternative to useState for complex state. A few less obvious behaviors:

subsections.heading: Basic pattern
Lazy initialization
subsections.content: The third argument to useReducer is an initializer function. It receives the second argument as input and is called once — useful for expensive initial computations or deriving initial state from props without running that logic on every render.

subsections.heading: dispatch is stable
subsections.content: Unlike state values, dispatch has a stable identity across renders. You don't need to include it in dependency arrays.

sections.title: Lesser-known facts about useMemo and useCallback
sections.description: These two hooks are often misunderstood or overused. Some things worth knowing:

subsections.heading: useMemo runs during render
subsections.content: Unlike useEffect, useMemo runs synchronously during rendering. Do not use it for side effects — use it only for pure computations.

subsections.heading: useCallback is syntactic sugar
subsections.content: useCallback(fn, deps) is equivalent to useMemo(() => fn, deps). It's just syntactic sugar for memoizing functions. Memoization has a cost. Don't add it everywhere — only when you have a measurable performance problem.

sections.title: useTransition
sections.description: Introduced in React 18, useTransition lets you mark certain state updates as non-urgent. React will prioritize urgent updates (like typing in an input) and defer the non-urgent ones (like filtering a large list), keeping the UI responsive.
paragraphs: The hook returns isPending — a boolean you can use to show a loading state while the transition is in progress — and startTransition, a function that wraps your non-urgent state update.

sections.title: Suspense and lazy
sections.description: Code splitting lets you split your bundle and load components on-demand. Use React.lazy for dynamic imports and Suspense to show a fallback while the component loads.
paragraphs: Suspense also works with data fetching in React 18+, when used with a Suspense-compatible data source (e.g. use(), SWR, React Query).

subsections.heading: Code splitting with lazy
subsections.content: The component is loaded only when it's first rendered.

subsections.heading: Suspense with data fetching

sections.title: useFormState and useOptimistic
sections.description: Server Actions in Next.js bring new possibilities for form handling. Two hooks make this ergonomic: useFormState for managing action state, and useOptimistic for instant UI feedback.

subsections.heading: useFormState for server actions
subsections.content: Lets you wire a form's action attribute directly to an async server function and capture its return value.

subsections.heading: useOptimistic for instant feedback
subsections.content: Show a pending version of your data immediately while the server request is in flight.
